person.prominent_name_reason: Major Benjamin Tallmadge Jr. (1754-1835) — spymaster of the Culper Ring, Washington's director of military intelligence, classmate of Nathan Hale (Yale 1773), custodian of Major André before his execution, hero of Battle of Fort St. George, U.S. Congressman (Federalist) 1801-1817, Society of the Cincinnati founding member. Grandson of Mehitable Hooker (H00109) and great-great-great-grandchild of Thomas Hooker. His wife Mary Floyd was the daughter of William Floyd, Signer of the Declaration of Independence.

political_offices.title: U.S. Representative
political_offices.notes: Succeeded William Edmond. Succeeded by Thomas Scott Williams.

orbit_connections.name: Gen. William Floyd
orbit_connections.relationship: Father-in-law — Benjamin Tallmadge married his daughter Mary Floyd Mar 18, 1784
orbit_connections.notes: William Floyd (Dec 17, 1734 – Aug 4, 1821). Continental Congressman; signer of the Declaration of Independence. Major General, New York Militia. His Setauket, Long Island estate was occupied by British forces during the Revolution — the same neighborhood where Tallmadge's Culper Spy Ring operated. Floyd was a neighbor, a comrade-in-arms, and became Tallmadge's father-in-law. (Wikipedia)

notable.notable_blurb: Continental Army spymaster and Connecticut Congressman

narrative_blocks.header: Director of Washington's Culper Spy Ring
narrative_blocks.detail: Appointed director of military intelligence by Washington, Tallmadge recruited Abraham Woodhull and Robert Townsend as key agents, operating under the alias "John Bolton." When Major John André was captured carrying Arnold's secret correspondence, Arnold fled to the British before he could be arrested. André was placed in Tallmadge's personal custody and executed Oct 2, 1780. Washington personally wrote of Tallmadge's intelligence operation: the Culper Ring has been called the most effective intelligence network of the American Revolution. His Yale classmate and close friend was Nathan Hale — executed as a spy by the British in 1776. Tallmadge was fighting the same war Hale died in. (Wikipedia; DAB; Society of the Cincinnati records)
narrative_blocks.body: Major Benjamin Tallmadge ran Washington's military intelligence network during the Revolutionary War, assembling the Culper Ring and playing the central role in exposing Benedict Arnold's plot to surrender West Point.

narrative_blocks.header: Raid on Fort St. George (1780)
narrative_blocks.detail: Washington personally wrote: "I have received with much pleasure the report of your successful enterprise upon Fort St. George." The raid was a textbook special operations mission — night crossing, surprise assault, clean withdrawal — conducted by the same man who was simultaneously running Washington's spy network. Tallmadge Hall at Fort Huachuca, Arizona (home of Army Intelligence) is named for him. The town of Tallmadge, Ohio bears his name. (Wikipedia; DAB)
narrative_blocks.body: Tallmadge led 80 men in whaleboats across Long Island Sound on November 21-22, 1780, captured and burned Fort St. George, and destroyed 300 tons of British hay — with no American casualties.

narrative_blocks.header: Married a Declaration signer's daughter
narrative_blocks.detail: His father Rev. Benjamin Tallmadge officiated at the wedding. William Floyd, whose daughter he married, had been a member of the Continental Congress and signed the Declaration; Floyd also served alongside Tallmadge in the Revolutionary War as a major general of New York militia. In Congress, Tallmadge was a committed Federalist. He was a founding member of the Society of the Cincinnati (Connecticut chapter, July 1783), an organization restricted to officers who served in the Revolution. "Continues to be represented by a living descendant in the society today." (Wikipedia; DAB; Society of the Cincinnati)
narrative_blocks.body: Tallmadge married Mary Floyd in 1784 — daughter of General William Floyd, signer of the Declaration of Independence — and served as a Connecticut Federalist Congressman from 1801 to 1817.

narrative_blocks.header: A Litchfield merchant and bank president
narrative_blocks.body: Settling in Litchfield after the war, Tallmadge grew wealthy in trade and land and led the local branch of the Phoenix Bank. He was among the town's first citizens for half a century.

bio.bio_blurb: Lt. colonel, 46th U.S. Infantry, War of 1812

narrative_blocks.header: A colonel in the War of 1812
narrative_blocks.body: William served as lieutenant colonel of the 46th United States Infantry in the War of 1812. He died unmarried at Leicester, New York, in 1822.

narrative_blocks.header: Named for an uncle who died in captivity
narrative_blocks.body: Benjamin and Mary's eldest son was named for his uncle William, captured at the Battle of Long Island and starved aboard a British prison ship. He later represented the family firm, B. Tallmadge and Company, in New York.

narrative_blocks.header: Laura died young
narrative_blocks.body: Laura died in 1816, aged about two.

notable.notable_blurb: Litchfield Female Academy; daughter of Culper Ring's Benjamin

narrative_blocks.header: Student at the Litchfield Female Academy
narrative_blocks.detail: Maxcy's friend Lemuel Williams wrote to him on Christmas Eve 1808, the week before his own wedding: 'O! my Virgil! How ardently I wish that we might all meet together, you & your Maria, I and my Sarah, in the bonds of wedded love.' The letter suggests Maria had already ended the engagement by then. Cushman and Maxcy had been classmates at Litchfield Law School — making the social circle of early American legal education small enough that broken hearts and new marriages overlapped by a season. (Galloway-Maxcy-Markoe Papers, Library of Congress; Litchfield Historical Society)
narrative_blocks.body: Maria Jones Tallmadge studied at the Litchfield Female Academy in 1801 and 1802. Before meeting John Cushman, she was engaged to Virgil Maxcy, a Litchfield Law School student. A surviving letter from Tapping Reeve consoling Maria suggests the break came around 1807 — after which John Cushman, who had just arrived at the same law school, became her husband in 1812.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter of Colonel Benjamin Tallmadge
narrative_blocks.detail: Benjamin Tallmadge is credited with uncovering the treason of Benedict Arnold and thwarting the British capture of West Point. He corresponded with his son-in-law John Cushman on legal matters throughout the 1820s. A 1824 letter survives in which Tallmadge wrote to Cushman about a domestic incident at his Litchfield home — a reminder that even the Culper Ring's spymaster kept up a lively family correspondence. (Litchfield Historical Society; Wikipedia on Benjamin Tallmadge)
narrative_blocks.body: Maria's father was Colonel Benjamin Tallmadge, Yale 1773, who ran General Washington's spy network — the Culper Ring — during the Revolutionary War. He later served as a U.S. Congressman. Her maternal grandfather was General William Floyd, member of the Continental Congress and signer of the Declaration of Independence.

cross_connections.display_label: is the mother of Edwards Williams, who married Mary Floyd Cushman

political_offices.title: Regent
political_offices.jurisdiction: University of the State of New York

notable.notable_blurb: U.S. Congressman, New York circuit judge, Litchfield Law

narrative_blocks.header: U.S. Congressman, circuit judge, and rector of Troy
narrative_blocks.detail: Cushman prepared for Yale at Plainfield Academy alongside John Adams (Yale 1795) and read law under Judge Zephaniah Swift of Windham before arriving at Litchfield. At Litchfield he became close friends with William L. Marcy — who went on to serve as U.S. Senator, Governor of New York, Secretary of War, and Secretary of State. Cushman's Litchfield Law School classmate John Allen Collier gave a memorial address at the opening of the Supreme Court after his death. He was also a trustee of Union College from 1833 until his death, and a regent of New York University. His father-in-law was Colonel Benjamin Tallmadge, Washington's spymaster. (Wikipedia; Litchfield Historical Society)
narrative_blocks.body: John Paine Cushman graduated Yale in 1807, studied at the Litchfield Law School in 1808, and was admitted to the bar in 1809. He was elected to the 15th Congress (1817-1819) as a Federalist, then served as Recorder of Troy (1834-1838) and Judge of the Third Circuit Court (1838-1844). Upon his death in 1848, the U.S. Supreme Court adjourned in his honor.

narrative_blocks.header: The Cushman Mansion
narrative_blocks.detail: The mansion remained in the family's orbit through much of the 19th century. Maria's father Colonel Benjamin Tallmadge was a frequent correspondent; their social circle included the leading legal and political figures of early New York State. (Landmark; YouTube documentary)
narrative_blocks.body: The Cushman family home at 39 First Street in Troy, built in 1820, is a four-story all-brick Federal-style house. Its original owners were direct descendants of Revolutionary War heroes and Plymouth Colony settlers.

institutions.institution_blurb: Attended 1808; admitted bar 1809; permanently settled Troy NY.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: His 1820 Troy townhouse, near the Washington Park he helped found.

narrative_blocks.header: Maria died young
narrative_blocks.body: Maria died in 1824, aged about four.

narrative_blocks.header: William died young
narrative_blocks.body: William died in 1832, in his first year.

notable.notable_blurb: U.S. Congressman and New York City police superintendent

cross_connections.display_label: his father-in-law, Judge Judson Canfield

career.role: Clerk of the New York Court of Appeals

narrative_blocks.header: He led New York City's police force
narrative_blocks.body: After terms as a New York alderman, state senator, and city recorder, Tallmadge served as Superintendent of the Metropolitan Police from 1857 to 1862. He had earlier sat one term in Congress as a Whig.

narrative_blocks.header: From Litchfield Law School to the bar
narrative_blocks.body: A Yale graduate of 1811, Tallmadge trained at the Litchfield Law School and practiced in New York City from 1813. He closed his career as clerk of the New York Court of Appeals.

narrative_blocks.header: Son of the Revolution's master spy
narrative_blocks.body: Frederick was a son of Benjamin Tallmadge, Washington's intelligence chief, and a grandson of the Declaration signer William Floyd. He returned to his native Litchfield to die in 1869.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: The 1901 Litchfield library building tied to the Noyes and Vanderpoel families.

notable.notable_blurb: Sons of the Revolution president; saved Fraunces Tavern

narrative_blocks.header: His gift saved Fraunces Tavern
narrative_blocks.body: As the Sons of the Revolution's second president, Tallmadge set his sights on the decaying Fraunces Tavern, where Washington had bidden his officers farewell. He signed the purchase contract on his deathbed in 1904, and his estate cleared the debt.

narrative_blocks.header: Great-grandson of two Revolutionary patriots
narrative_blocks.body: Tallmadge descended from the Declaration signer William Floyd and from Benjamin Tallmadge of the Culper Spy Ring. A New York lawyer, he belonged to the Union Club, the New-York Historical Society, and the Society of the Cincinnati.

narrative_blocks.header: He left the society his library and relics
narrative_blocks.body: In his will Tallmadge gave the Sons of the Revolution his library and Revolutionary relics, among them Benjamin Tallmadge's handwritten memoir. A bronze tablet by Victor Brenner marks Fraunces Tavern as his memorial.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: The Revolutionary tavern his bequest rescued for the Sons of the Revolution.

bio.bio_blurb: Sons of the Revolution president; saved Fraunces Tavern

parents.father_research_notes: John Delafield (1748-1824), English-American merchant and diplomat

notable.notable_blurb: Survived mutiny at sea; chartered NY's first agricultural

narrative_blocks.header: Mutiny at 22
narrative_blocks.detail: Continuing north, the ship met a violent storm off France and limped into Bristol, England. There, with Europe in the grip of the Napoleonic Wars and Anglo-American tensions rising toward the War of 1812, Delafield was thrown into prison on an alleged customs violation. He was released but confined within 30 miles of Bristol — 'a stranger without money.' He worked for a cabinet maker and a drug store, married a Bristol woman, and remained under British surveillance until the war's end. When his wife died in 1820 he returned to New York. (Rose Hill Mansion historical article; Willers 1900)
narrative_blocks.body: In 1808, John Delafield was sent as super-cargo on a merchant brig to the West Indies. The captain died of yellow fever in Cuba. The mate died two days after leaving Havana.

narrative_blocks.header: Co-organized the New York Philharmonic
narrative_blocks.detail: In 1843 he retired from banking and purchased 352 acres in Seneca County, New York, naming his farm 'Oaklands.' He embraced the new drain tile technology introduced by neighbor John Johnston and became a leading voice for agricultural modernization. His farm was named best in Seneca County (1846) and best in New York State (1848). He was elected president of the Seneca County Agricultural Society and in 1851 became president of the New York State Agricultural Society. (Rose Hill Mansion historical article)
narrative_blocks.body: Back in New York by 1820, Delafield rose from bank teller to president of the Phoenix Bank by 1830. He was an early promoter of the Hudson River Railroad, a director of the University of New York, and helped organize the Philharmonic Musical Society — which became the New York Philharmonic.

narrative_blocks.header: Chartered New York's first state agricultural college
narrative_blocks.detail: He died suddenly on October 22, 1853 — six months after the charter passed. The Syracuse Daily Journal wrote: 'There was probably no man in the State more deeply interested in the progress of Agricultural science and reform than Mr. Delafield, and his labors to urge it forward were arduous and incessant.' The college at Oaklands never advanced after his death. He was survived by his second wife Harriet Wadsworth Tallmadge, daughter of Colonel Benjamin Tallmadge (H00757) — Washington's Culper Ring spymaster. (Willers 1900; Syracuse Daily Journal, Oct 26, 1853)
narrative_blocks.body: On April 15, 1853, the New York State Legislature passed 'An Act to Incorporate The New-York State Agricultural College,' chartered at Delafield's Oaklands farm. It may have been the first state agricultural college in the United States. Delafield was named president of its board of trustees.

narrative_blocks.header: He died of brain fever off Gibraltar
narrative_blocks.body: A lieutenant in the United States Navy, Tallmadge died of brain fever aboard the frigate Brandywine near Gibraltar in 1831, still unmarried. He had received an honorary master's degree from Yale only the year before.

bio.bio_blurb: U.S. Navy lieutenant; died near Gibraltar

military_service.notes: Died of brain fever aboard ship near Gibraltar in 1831.

burial.plot_notes: Setauket Presbyterian Church Cemetery, Setauket NY. SOURCE: research notes.

narrative_blocks.header: Her son became Washington's spymaster
narrative_blocks.body: Susannah Smith married Rev. Benjamin Tallmadge of Setauket. Their son Benjamin grew up to run the Culper spy ring for General Washington during the Revolution.

burial.plot_notes: First Presbyterian Church Cemetery, White Plains NY; later church construction extended over his grave. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

notable.notable_blurb: Physician-minister at White Plains for thirty years

cross_connections.display_label: Young friend in New York who drew him toward the ministry

career.notes: Practiced medicine alongside ministry for nearly 30 years. 'Said to have been a very successful physician... remarkably successful in cases of insanity.'
Served the White Plains church which was destroyed in the Battle of White Plains October 1776, seven years after his death.

narrative_blocks.header: Walked the Hudson with Jonathan Edwards as friend
narrative_blocks.body: He was a personal friend of Jonathan Edwards, the most important American theologian of the eighteenth century. On one occasion, walking together along the banks of the Hudson, Smith confided to Edwards: 'To be separated from my endeared friends and companions are the most bitter trials of my life.' The friendship spanned Smith's quarter-century at White Plains and Edwards's career through Northampton, Stockbridge, and Princeton.

narrative_blocks.header: Physician and Presbyterian minister for thirty years
narrative_blocks.body: He practiced medicine and ministry simultaneously at Rye and White Plains for nearly thirty years — a rare dual vocation in colonial America. Contemporaries described him as 'remarkably successful in cases of insanity.' His Yale class of 1727 trained him for ministry; the medicine he taught himself.

narrative_blocks.header: His church destroyed at Battle of White Plains
narrative_blocks.body: Seven years after his death in 1771, the Battle of White Plains was fought in front of his Presbyterian church on Oct 28, 1776 — and the building was destroyed. A second church on the same ground was later destroyed by fire. The third — built of gray stone — was eventually extended over his grave, and the original upright slab was moved further back.

narrative_blocks.header: A church was later built over his grave
narrative_blocks.body: Rev. Dr. John Smith served nearly thirty years as Presbyterian minister at Rye and White Plains. After his death the White Plains meetinghouse was rebuilt over his burial spot, and his gravestone was moved back to accommodate it.

sources.short: Smith-Hooker Genealogy by Della Bishop, 1936, Ithaca NY

cross_connections.display_label: Mehitable Hooker Smith is the great-grandmother of Maj. Benjamin Tallmadge.

narrative_blocks.header: Twelve children with Rev. John Smith of Rye
narrative_blocks.body: She married Rev. John Smith of Rye, New York, and had twelve children between 1727 and 1750. The family lived in Westchester County during the years just before the Revolution turned that border country into a no-man's land.

narrative_blocks.header: Great-grandmother of Washington's spymaster
narrative_blocks.body: Her great-grandson Major Benjamin Tallmadge ran the Culper Ring for George Washington — the network that exposed Benedict Arnold's 1780 treason. Tallmadge was a classmate of Nathan Hale and a fellow Hooker descendant.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter Abigail at Washington's White Plains headquarters
narrative_blocks.body: Her daughter Abigail Smith married Jacob Purdy, whose Purchase, NY house served as Washington's headquarters during the Battle of White Plains in 1776 and again in 1778. The Purdy descendants would carry the line through several Patriot–Loyalist conflicts.

narrative_blocks.header: She and her sister married two Smith brothers
narrative_blocks.body: Mehitable Hooker married Rev. Dr. John Smith of Rye. Her elder sister Hannah had married his brother Thomas, so two Hooker sisters wed two Smith brothers.

burial.plot_notes: First Presbyterian Church Cemetery, White Plains NY. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

INST022.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.notes: First American foreign-mission organization. Tree connections: Rev. Elias Cornelius (agent 1816, secretary 1831-32); Ainsworth and Harriet Ellsworth Blunt, missionaries to the Cherokee at Brainerd, Tennessee; Eliza Talcott, missionary to Japan; Rev. William Banfield Capron, Madura mission; Judge John Hooker, member.

INST0045.name: Connecticut Land Company
INST0045.notes: Formed 1795 by 36 Connecticut investors who purchased the entire Connecticut Western Reserve from the State of Connecticut for over $1,000,000 — land the state had 'reserved' for citizens whose property was damaged by the British in the Revolution. In April 1807, Richfield Township was acquired for $26,087 by seven men: Col. Benjamin Tallmadge (H00757), Solomon Lathrop, Jeremiah Wilcox, Uriel Holmes Jr., Pierpont Edwards (H00386), Daniel Green, and Jonathan Dwight Sr. — two of the seven being Hooker descendants. Pierpont Edwards (H00386) invested $60,000 — approximately 5% of total company holdings. His son John Stark Edwards (HD2044) served as his first agent in the Reserve, but died Feb 22, 1813 in Warren, Trumbull County OH age 35 — the year Whittlesey rode through the black swamp and nearly died at Fort Meigs. Pierpont Township, Ashtabula County, Ohio is named for Edwards. In 1811, Heman Oviatt (husband of HD2934 Eunice Newton Oviatt) bought the northwest quarter of Richfield Township from Col. Tallmadge for $1.25 an acre. John Newton (HD2925) arrived in Richfield in the early 1820s and purchased most of the east center land, building the home later owned by Homer Carter. Elisha Whittlesey (X01997) arrived in Canfield June 27, 1806 — the same summer the company was active — and became public prosecutor for the entire Western Reserve. The company was ultimately a financial failure. (Richfield Township History; Ohio History Journal; Whittlesey Genealogy #310)

INST163.name: B. Tallmadge and Company
INST163.notes: Dry-goods firm founded by Benjamin Tallmadge in Litchfield; his eldest son William Smith Tallmadge represented it in New York.
